findings.reachability: Any caller of `encrypt()` or `encryptKeyInfo()` in xmlenc.js that controls `options.pem` or any caller that invokes the template directly with a controlled `encryptionPublicCert` parameter; in SAML/federation scenarios this can be the identity provider certificate supplied at runtime.
findings.justification: Unlike `keyEncryptionMethod`, `keyEncryptionDigest`, and `encryptedKey` which are all wrapped with `escapehtml()`, `encryptionPublicCert` is interpolated raw into the XML template at line 10, enabling an attacker who controls the PEM certificate or the template parameter directly to inject arbitrary XML nodes into the `<KeyInfo>` element and alter document structure.

detection_pattern.section_name: XML injection: raw template interpolation in XML output
detection_pattern.description: Finds template literal interpolations in XML-generating files; when a field is interpolated without an XML/HTML escaping wrapper (e.g. escapehtml, xmlescape) an attacker controlling that field can inject arbitrary XML nodes or attributes.

findings.location: module.exports template literal, line 7 ‚Äî DigestMethod Algorithm attribute
findings.reachability: Any caller that controls `options.keyEncryptionDigest` ‚Äî a config-time or caller-supplied string that is concatenated directly after the fixed URI prefix `http://www.w3.org/2000/09/xmldsig#` with only HTML escaping applied.
findings.justification: The DigestMethod Algorithm URI is constructed as a string concatenation of a hardcoded prefix and `escapehtml(keyEncryptionDigest)` with no allowlist validation, so an attacker supplying an unexpected digest value can craft an arbitrary URI (e.g. referencing an attacker-controlled algorithm) as the Algorithm attribute, though HTML-escaping prevents attribute breakout.

detection_pattern.section_name: Algorithm URI injection: unvalidated digest algorithm
detection_pattern.description: Finds XML Algorithm attribute constructions that interpolate a caller-supplied digest or algorithm name without an allowlist check; permits an attacker to specify unsupported or attacker-chosen cryptographic algorithm URIs.
